topic.what: Foundational pharmacological study demonstrating gastroprotective effects of Zingiber officinale (ginger) rhizome extract in multiple rat ulcer models, including ethanol-, aspirin-, and stress-induced ulceration.
topic.how: Rat ulcer models (ethanol, HCl/ethanol, aspirin, cold-restraint stress) used to evaluate ginger extract and fractions; gastric lesion index, ulcer score, and mucus content measured; comparison against carbenoxolone and sucralfate controls.
topic.why_matters: Ginger is among the most universally used medicinal plants across Ayurvedic, Chinese, Greek, Arabic, and folk traditions for GI complaints; this early study provides the pharmacological anchor for decades of subsequent research and validates cross-cultural empirical use with experimental evidence.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in gastric lesion score in ethanol ulcer model
characteristics.value: Significant reduction in ulcer index at 500 mg/kg dose
characteristics.evidence: Dose-response across 250 and 500 mg/kg ginger extract
characteristics.context: Wistar rats; reproducible across multiple ulcer-induction protocols

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in gastric lesion score under NSAID challenge
characteristics.value: Moderate reduction; less pronounced than ethanol model
characteristics.context: Suggests partial cytoprotective mechanism rather than prostaglandin-mediated

characteristics.what_it_measures: Gastric mucus glycoprotein content after treatment
characteristics.value: Increased gastric mucus in ginger-treated animals
characteristics.evidence: Alcian blue mucus staining and hexosamine assay
characteristics.context: Points to mucosal barrier enhancement as mechanism

good_for: Validating ethnopharmacological basis for universal ginger use in GI disorders
Identifying mechanisms (mucus enhancement, cytoprotection) underlying ginger's gastroprotective effects
Foundational reference for ginger as a safe dietary gastroprotective adjunct
Cross-cultural concept: same plant validated across Ayurveda, TCM, and Western folk medicine
not_for: H. pylori eradication (not an antimicrobial study)
Clinical dosing guidance (animal doses not directly translatable)
Patients on anticoagulants (ginger's antiplatelet effects are a separate concern)
requires: Human clinical trials for gastroprotective dosing
Isolation of specific active gingerol/shogaol fractions responsible for mucosa protection
Differentiation of gastroprotective vs anti-emetic mechanisms

novelty: One of the earliest systematic pharmacological studies of ginger gastroprotection using multiple ulcer models in parallel
Establishes mucus-enhancing mechanism as distinct from prostaglandin-synthesis pathway used by many standard gastroprotectants
Provides the experimental anchor for ginger's inclusion in gastritis ethnopharmacology reviews across traditions
open_problems: Specific phytochemical fractions (gingerols, shogaols, paradols) responsible for gastroprotection not individually characterized in this study
Long-term safety and efficacy in chronic gastritis vs acute ulcer models not addressed
Interaction with H. pylori-associated gastritis not studied
Translation to human bioavailability and effective gastric concentration unknown
